title: Python SQL Setup Guide
text:
1 Python Enterprise CRUD Demo
This guide introduces a small enterprise application built with Python, Streamlit, and Microsoft SQL Server.
The goal is not to learn every technical detail at once. Start with the small practice repositories, check that your setup works, and then move to the full CRUD application.

title: Python SQL Setup Guide
text:
1.1 Learning Path
Follow the chapters in this order:



Step
What you do
Where




1
Prepare your SQL Server credentials
Credentials


2
Install the required tools
Windows Setup or macOS Setup


3
Test the small practice repositories
Practice Repositories


4
Understand the database connection
Server Connection


5
Run and inspect the Streamlit app
Streamlit Application

title: Python SQL Setup Guide
text:
1.2 Repositories
The course uses three GitHub repositories:



Repository
Purpose




mssql-example
Test a basic Python connection to SQL Server


streamlit-mssql
Run a small Streamlit app with SQL Server data


sopra
Work with the full layered CRUD application



Keep the repositories in separate folders. Each repository is a separate learning step.

title: Python SQL Setup Guide
text:
1.3 What This Project Demonstrates
The full application manages quantity-based B2B discounts. It shows how a typical Python application can:

read from and write to Microsoft SQL Server
keep database credentials out of the source code
separate the user interface from business logic and database access
use database views for controlled enterprise data access

title: Python SQL Setup Guide
text:
1.4 Architecture At A Glance





flowchart LR
    U["Browser"] --> A["Streamlit UI"]
    A --> B["Business Logic"]
    B --> C["Database Access"]
    C --> D["SQL Server Views"]
    D --> E["SQL Server"]






You do not need to understand every layer before starting. The important idea is that each layer has one clear responsibility.

title: Python SQL Setup Guide
text:
1.5 What To Focus On First
When working through the material, focus on these questions:

Can Python connect to the SQL Server database?
Can Streamlit display data from SQL Server in the browser?
Where does the full application handle user input, business rules, and SQL access?

If one step fails, fix that step before moving on. This keeps setup problems separate from application design problems.

title: 2  Credentials
text:
2.1 Create the .env File
Before you run the application, prepare the local credential file.
In your local copy of the repository, create a file named .env in the root folder. This file will hold the credentials for connecting to the SQL Server database.
Replace the placeholder values (foo) with the actual credentials provided for your SQL Server instance. The file should look like this:
Make sure to keep the .env file secure and do not share it with others, as it contains sensitive information.
Dont forget to add .env to your .gitignore file if you are using Git, to prevent it from being accidentally committed to version control.

title: 2  Credentials
text:
MSSQL_SERVER=foo
MSSQL_DATABASE=foo
MSSQL_USERNAME=foo
MSSQL_PASSWORD=foo
APP_USER=foo

MSSQL_DRIVER=ODBC Driver 18 for SQL Server
SQL_ENCRYPT=yes
TRUST_SERVER_CERTIFICATE=true








Do not hard-code database passwords in app.py, sql_example.py, or repository files. In enterprise projects, this is considered a serious security mistake.

title: 3  Windows
text:
3.1 Basic requirements
If you are using Windows, follow these steps.
First, make sure your system is up to date.
Next, enable Developer Mode:

title: 3  Windows
text:
Open Settings > Windows Update.



Open Settings > Update & Security > For developers.
Toggle Developer Mode and accept the disclaimer.

title: 3  Windows
text:
3.2 Command-line basics
We use Git Bash as our terminal. Download the latest Git for Windows installer from git-scm.com and complete the setup (Git Bash is included). After installation, open Git Bash from the Start menu.






NoteWhy Git Bash?





Git Bash provides a Unix-like terminal experience on Windows, making it easier to follow along with commands that are commonly used in macOS and Linux environments.



Keep the terminal window open while you work through the chapters.

3.2.1 uv
We use uv, a Python package manager that is extremely fast. It replaces pip, poetry, and virtualenv.
To install uv, follow this guide

title: 3  Windows
text:
3.3 Server Tools
The Microsoft ODBC Driver for SQL Server enables native connectivity from Windows applications to Microsoft SQL Server and Azure SQL databases.
Follow the steps below to install the ODBC Driver (version 18) and command-line tools on your Windows machine:

Installation Instructions

For most users, the “Download Microsoft ODBC Driver 18 for SQL Server (x64)” installer is the right choice.

title: 3  Windows
text:
3.4 Verify the Tools
After the installations, check the most important commands:
uv --version
python --version
sqlcmd -?

title: 3  Windows
text:
3.5 Install the Python Packages
Inside the project folder, run:
uv sync
This command creates the local virtual environment and installs all packages from pyproject.toml and uv.lock.

title: 4  macOS
text:
4.1 Basic requirements
If you are using Mac, follow these steps.
First, make sure your system is up to date.
Open System Settings > General > Software Update.

title: 4  macOS
text:
4.2 Command-line basics
Launch Terminal (Spotlight search for “Terminal”).
Commands in this book assume the zsh shell, which is the default on modern macOS systems.
Keep the terminal window open while you work through the chapters.

title: 4  macOS
text:
4.3 Xcode
Apple’s Xcode command line tools are required to build and run some of the code.
Install Xcode command line tools:
xcode-select --install
If you get the following message, you can skip this step:
xcode-select: note: Command line tools are already installed. Use “Software Update” in System Settings or the softwareupdate command line interface to install updates

title: 4  macOS
text:
4.4 Package manager
Homebrew downloads applications, command line tools, and libraries with one command. You avoid manual downloads and it keeps everything organized.

4.4.1 Install Homebrew

Open Terminal and run the official installer:

/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"

Enter your password when prompted (you won’t see any characters while typing).
Press ENTER to continue
When the script finishes, follow the on-screen instructions to add Homebrew to your shell profile (this step is required for brew to be found).
Close the Terminal window.
Open a new Terminal, then verify the installation:

brew --version


4.4.2 Update Homebrew
If you already have Homebrew installed, make sure everything is up to date:
brew update
Use brew upgrade to actually update installed packages.
brew upgrade

title: 4  macOS
text:
4.5 uv
We use uv, a Python package manager that is extremely fast. It replaces pip, poetry, and virtualenv.
To install uv, follow this guide

title: 4  macOS
text:
4.6 Server Tools
We need to install some drivers for Microsoft SQL Server.

4.6.1 Install Server Tools
Open your Terminal and run:
brew tap microsoft/mssql-release https://github.com/Microsoft/homebrew-mssql-release
ACCEPT_EULA=Y brew install msodbcsql18 mssql-tools unixodbc

msodbcsql18 is the latest Microsoft ODBC Driver for SQL Server.
mssql-tools installs sqlcmd and bcp for command-line access.
unixodbc provides the shared ODBC libraries Python will load.







The ACCEPT_EULA=Y prefix lets the installer know you accept the Microsoft end-user license agreement. Without it the install stops halfway through.




Close and reopen Terminal.



4.6.2 Test the installation
Confirm that the ODBC driver registered correctly:
odbcinst -j
Look for lines referencing Driver Manager: unixODBC.
Your Mac is now ready to connect to SQL Server databases from VS Code and Python.


4.6.3 Troubleshooting
Some shells require an extra step to expose the SQL Server utilities. Add the following line to ~/.zshrc or ~/.bash_profile if sqlcmd is not found after reopening Terminal:
export PATH="/opt/homebrew/opt/msodbcsql18/bin:/opt/homebrew/opt/mssql-tools/bin:$PATH"
Intel Macs should use /usr/local/opt/ instead of /opt/homebrew/opt/.
Reload your shell:
source ~/.zshrc

title: 4  macOS
text:
4.7 Install the Python Packages
Inside the project folder, run:
uv sync
This command creates the local virtual environment and installs all Python dependencies for the demo application.

title: 5  Practice Repositories
text:
5.1 Repository 1: SQL Server Connection
Before working in the full project, we use two smaller repositories to check the technical basics.
The goal is to separate setup problems from application design problems:
Use this repository first:
With Git, use the following commands in your terminal (one at a time):
This repository is intentionally small. It focuses on:
The SOPRA example script reads the discount overview:
It also reads the customer list of values used later by the Streamlit dropdown:
If this step fails, fix the driver, credentials, network access, or SOPRA database permissions before continuing.

title: 5  Practice Repositories
text:
Open mssql-example on GitHub
Download mssql-example as ZIP


git clone https://github.com/kirenz/mssql-example.git
cd mssql-example
uv sync
cp .env.example .env
uv run python sql_example_sopra.py


loading database credentials from .env
building a SQLAlchemy connection
running a simple SQL query against the SOPRA views
reading the result into a pandas DataFrame


SELECT TOP (5)
    RabattID,
    Kunde,
    MengeVon,
    MengeBis,
    RabattProzent,
    GiltVon,
    GiltBis
FROM list_views.V_LIST_B2B_DISCOUNT
ORDER BY RabattID DESC;

SELECT TOP (5)
    CUSTOMER_ID,
    CUSTOMER_LONG
FROM dbo.LOV_CUSTOMER
ORDER BY CUSTOMER_ID;

title: 5  Practice Repositories
text:
5.2 Repository 2: Streamlit and SQL Server
Use this repository after the plain SQL connection works:

Open streamlit-mssql on GitHub
Download streamlit-mssql as ZIP

With Git:
git clone https://github.com/kirenz/streamlit-mssql.git
cd streamlit-mssql
uv sync
cp .env.example .env
uv run streamlit run app.py
This repository adds the browser interface. It focuses on:

starting a Streamlit app
connecting Streamlit to SQL Server
entering and running a SQL query against the SOPRA views
displaying the result as a table

If this step works, the basic Streamlit and SQL Server setup is ready.

title: 5  Practice Repositories
text:
5.3 Repository 3: Full CRUD Application
Use the main repository after the two practice steps:

Open sopra on GitHub
Download sopra as ZIP

With Git:
git clone https://github.com/kirenz/sopra.git
cd sopra
uv sync
cp .env.example .env
uv run python sql_example.py
uv run streamlit run app.py
The main project introduces the application architecture:

app.py handles the Streamlit user interface
services/ contains business rules and validation
repositories/ contains SQL Server access
models/ contains domain data structures







Each repository has its own .env file. Credentials stay local on your machine and are not committed to GitHub.

title: 6  VS Code
text:
6.1 Install VS Code
Visual Studio Code acts as your code editor, terminal, and database explorer. In this project, it is useful for both Python development and direct SQL inspection.
If you haven’t already, install Visual Studio Code:
Launch VS Code once to allow it to finish installing components.

title: 6  VS Code
text:
macOS: Download the from code.visualstudio.com and drag Visual Studio Code into Applications.
Windows: Download the installer and click through the setup, leaving the default options enabled (add to PATH, register the code command, etc.).

title: 6  VS Code
text:
6.2 Essential extensions
Open the Extensions view and install:

Python (ms-python.python) – language support and linting.
SQL Server (mssql) (ms-mssql.mssql) – database connections and query runner.
vscode-icons (vscode-icons-team.vscode-icons) – optional, but helpful for identifying files quickly.

title: 6  VS Code
text:
6.3 Enable the code command

6.3.1 macOS Terminal

Launch VS Code, press Cmd+Shift+P, and run Shell Command: Install ‘code’ command in PATH.
Close any open Terminal windows, then open a new one and run

code --version
to confirm the CLI is available.

From the folder you want to open, run

code .
to launch VS Code in that location.


6.3.2 Windows

From the folder you want to open, run this in Git Bash

code .
to launch VS Code in that location.

If the command is missing, add VS Code’s bin directory to your Git Bash PATH by appending the snippet below to ~/.bashrc (create the file if it doesn’t exist):

    VS_CODE="/c/Users/$USERNAME/AppData/Local/Programs/Microsoft VS Code/bin"
    if [ -d "$VS_CODE" ] && [[ ":$PATH:" != *":$VS_CODE:"* ]]; then
      PATH="$VS_CODE:$PATH"
    fi

Reload Git Bash

source ~/.bashrc

Try again:

code .

title: 6  VS Code
text:
6.4 Integrated terminal tips

Open the integrated terminal in VS Code. On Windows, set Git Bash as the default shell so every VS Code terminal matches the rest of this guide.
Set your default terminal profile via Terminal > Configure Default Profile… so that VS Code opens the same shell you used during installation.

title: 6  VS Code
text:
6.5 Create the SQL Server Connection
Use the SQL Server extension to create a saved connection profile.

Open the SQL Server view.
Click Add Connection.
Fill in the connection dialog:

Server name: edu.hdm-server.eu
Authentication type: SQL Login
User name: use your course login
Password: use the matching password
Database name: ERPDEV
Trust server certificate: true

Save the profile so you can reuse it later.

title: 6  VS Code
text:
6.6 First Query
After the connection is established, create a new SQL file and run:
SELECT TOP (10)
    RabattID,
    Kunde,
    MengeVon,
    MengeBis,
    RabattProzent,
    GiltVon,
    GiltBis
FROM list_views.V_LIST_B2B_DISCOUNT
ORDER BY RabattID DESC;
If you see rows in the results grid, your VS Code setup is ready.

title: 7  Server Connection
text:
7.1 The Connection Settings
We connect to SQL Server from Python with four building blocks:
The project stores the database configuration in .env:

title: 7  Server Connection
text:
MSSQL_SERVER=...
MSSQL_DATABASE=...
MSSQL_USERNAME=...
MSSQL_PASSWORD=...
MSSQL_DRIVER=ODBC Driver 18 for SQL Server
TRUST_SERVER_CERTIFICATE=true
APP_USER=...

title: 7  Server Connection
section: 7.2 Why the Connection Logic Lives in repositories/db.py
text:
7.2 Why the Connection Logic Lives in repositories/db.py
repositories/db.py has two responsibilities:

load and validate the environment variables
create one shared SQLAlchemy engine for the application

This keeps the rest of the project clean:

the Streamlit UI does not need to know how ODBC strings work
the service layer does not need to know where credentials come from
the repository layer can focus on SQL statements only

title: 7  Server Connection
text:
7.3 How the Connection String Works
The SQLAlchemy engine uses the dialect mssql+pyodbc, which means:

SQLAlchemy handles connection management and parameterized SQL
pyodbc uses the installed Microsoft ODBC Driver 18 underneath
the actual server communication still happens through the native SQL Server driver

title: 7  Server Connection
text:
7.4 Why We Still Use pandas
pandas.read_sql(...) is convenient for read operations because the result is immediately available as a DataFrame. That is useful for:

displaying tables in Streamlit
filtering data
checking business rules, such as overlapping discounts

For writes, the repository uses parameterized SQL statements with sqlalchemy.text(...), because that is clearer and safer for INSERT and UPDATE.

title: 7  Server Connection
text:
7.5 Next Step
In the next chapter, you will run the standalone script sql_example.py and inspect the exact SQL views that power the CRUD application.

title: 8  Streamlit Application
text:
8.1 Start the App
The file app.py is the presentation layer of the project. It contains the Streamlit user interface and deliberately avoids direct SQL statements.
After the two practice repositories work, return to the full sopra project and run:

title: 8  Streamlit Application
text: uv run streamlit run app.py

title: 8  Streamlit Application
text:
8.2 User Interface Structure
The application uses three tabs:

List Discounts
Create Discount
Edit Discount

This mirrors a classic CRUD workflow and makes the mapping between UI actions and backend logic very explicit.

title: 8  Streamlit Application
text:
8.3 Layer Responsibilities

8.3.1 Presentation Layer
app.py is responsible for:

rendering Streamlit widgets
collecting user input
showing success and error messages
calling the service layer



8.3.2 Business Logic Layer
services/discount_service.py is responsible for:

validating quantities, dates, and discount percentages
preventing overlapping discounts for the same customer
translating repository errors into business-oriented messages



8.3.3 Data Access Layer
repositories/discount_repo.py is responsible for:

reading the overview and LOV data with pandas.read_sql(...)
inserting rows through ins_views.V_INS_B2B_DISCOUNT
updating rows through upd_views.V_UPD_B2B_DISCOUNT
isolating SQL-specific details from the rest of the code

title: 8  Streamlit Application
text:
8.4 Why This Separation Matters
This design makes it easier to reason about the application:

if the SQL is wrong, you inspect the repository
if the business rule is wrong, you inspect the service
if the layout or form flow is wrong, you inspect the Streamlit UI

That is exactly why layered architectures are common in enterprise systems.

title: 8  Streamlit Application
text:
8.5 Error Handling
The application demonstrates two important error categories:

local validation errors, such as invalid quantity ranges
database errors returned by SQL Server

Students should notice that both categories are shown to the user, but they originate in different layers.

title: 8  Streamlit Application
text:
8.6 Suggested Exercises

add a delete workflow with a separate database view
add unit tests for the validation rules
add more business rules, for example maximum discount thresholds by customer type
add a detail page or KPI cards above the table
